title: US Choropleth Maps
text:
## Introduction
This page shows choropleth maps using state-level data.

library(tidyverse)
library(maps)
library(sf)
library(leaflet)
library(htmltools)
library(poliscidata)
library(stringr)
library(viridis)

states_polygon <- as_tibble(map_data("state")) |>
  select(region, group, order, lat, long)

states_sf <- read_sf("https://rstudio.github.io/leaflet/json/us-states.geojson") |>
  select(name, geometry)

polisci_data <- as_tibble(poliscidata::states) |>
  select(state, carfatal07, cook_index3)

states_polygon2 <- states_polygon |>
  mutate(region = str_to_lower(str_squish(region)))

states_sf2 <- states_sf |>
  mutate(state_name = str_to_lower(str_squish(name)))

polisci_data2 <- polisci_data |>
  mutate(state_name = str_to_lower(str_squish(state)))

states_static <- states_polygon2 |>
  left_join(polisci_data2, by = c("region" = "state_name")) |>
  filter(!(region %in% c("alaska", "hawaii")))

states_interactive <- states_sf2 |>
  left_join(polisci_data2, by = "state_name") |>
  filter(!(state_name %in% c("alaska", "hawaii")))

## Numeric Variable: Car Fatality Rate

ggplot(states_static, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group)) +
  geom_polygon(aes(fill = carfatal07), color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_map() +
  theme_void() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Car Fatality Rate")








This map shows how car fatality rates vary across states. Higher values appear in parts of the South, while many northern states tend to have lower rates. This suggests there may be regional differences in driving conditions, road safety, or behavior.


pal_num <- colorNumeric("viridis", states_interactive$carfatal07)

labels_num <- lapply(
  paste0(states_interactive$name, ": ", round(states_interactive$carfatal07, 2)),
  HTML
)

leaflet(states_interactive) |>
  setView(-96, 37.8, 4) |>
  addTiles() |>
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal_num(carfatal07),
    weight = 2,
    color = "white",
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    label = labels_num
  )





This interactive map allows users to hover over each state to see the exact car fatality rate, making it easier to compare values across states.

## Categorical Variable: Cook Index

states_static <- states_static |>
  mutate(cook_index3 = factor(cook_index3))

ggplot(states_static, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group)) +
  geom_polygon(aes(fill = cook_index3), color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_map() +
  theme_void() +
  scale_fill_brewer(name = "Cook Index", palette = "Set1")








This map shows the political category of each state using the Cook Index. We can see clear regional patterns, with some areas leaning more Democratic and others more Republican, while a few states are more balanced.


pal_cat <- colorFactor("Set1", states_interactive$cook_index3)

labels_cat <- lapply(
  paste0(states_interactive$name, ": ", states_interactive$cook_index3),
  HTML
)

leaflet(states_interactive) |>
  setView(-96, 37.8, 4) |>
  addTiles() |>
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal_cat(cook_index3),
    weight = 2,
    color = "white",
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    label = labels_cat
  )





This interactive map allows users to explore the political category of each state by hovering over it.

title: Mini-Project 1: Text Analysis
text:
For Mini-Project 1, I analyzed immigration-related tweets. Social media platforms like Twitter are important places where people express opinions about immigration, refugees, asylum, borders, and migration.
The goal of this project was to identify common themes, word patterns, and emotional tone in the tweets.

title: Mini-Project 1: Text Analysis
text:
For Mini-Project 1, I analyzed immigration-related tweets. Social media platforms like Twitter are important places where people express opinions about immigration, refugees, asylum, borders, and migration.
The goal of this project was to identify common themes, word patterns, and emotional tone in the tweets.

title: Mini-Project 1: Text Analysis
text:
Data
The dataset includes a random sample of immigration-related tweets. I cleaned the tweet text by changing it to lowercase, removing links, and identifying migration-related words.

title: Mini-Project 1: Text Analysis
text:
Methods
I used text analysis tools in R, including stringr, tidytext, and sentiment analysis. I used regular expressions to remove links and identify words related to immigration, refugees, migrants, asylum, and borders.

title: Mini-Project 1: Text Analysis
text:
Results

Most Common Words









This plot shows the most common words in the tweets. Words such as “immigration,” “uk,” and “immigrants” appear frequently.


Sentiment Analysis









This plot shows that negative words appear more often than positive words.


Most Common Sentiment Words









This plot shows the most common positive and negative words in the tweets.

title: Mini-Project 1: Text Analysis
text:
Conclusion
Overall, this project shows that text analysis can help reveal patterns in social media discussions about immigration. The tweets often included strong opinions, and negative language appeared more frequently than positive language. However, some positive language related to reform and support was also present.

title: Aitha Iriho
text:
Hello! My name is Aitha Iriho.
I am a student at St. Olaf College studying Statistics & Data Science and French.
I am interested in data analysis, statistical modeling, and applying data to real-world problems.
